VO_EN: You don't arrest people.
You collect them.
Right now there are fourteen names on your phone.
Six of them exist on paper. The department knows about those.
The rest don't exist at all.
Same charge. Different outcome.
Your choice.
They teach you about confidential informants in the academy.
There's a whole briefing on it. PowerPoint slides. Protocol charts on the wall.
The instructor calls them assets.
Asset management. Asset deployment. Asset protection.
You write it down like it's a vocabulary test.
It sounds clean. Organized.
Nobody mentions the word people.
First patrol.
You arrest a guy with half a gram tucked in his coat lining.
Book him. Process him. Photograph him. File the report.
Takes you an hour and a half for the paperwork alone.
By the time you finish, the shift's almost over.
Your sergeant looks at the whiteboard.
One arrest.
He doesn't say anything. Doesn't even look at you.
But you hear it.
A detective pulls you aside after the next shift.
Senior guy. Fifteen years in narcotics. Jacket so wrinkled it looks like he slept in it.
He walks you to the parking garage. Doesn't say why.
Then he reaches into his jacket and pulls out a phone.
Not the department phone. A different one.
Prepaid. No case number. No serial log. No chain of custody.
He flips it open. Contacts list. Just numbers. No names.
This is how you get numbers.
He says it the way someone explains where the supply closet is.
You watch him do it for the first time.
Booking room. Fluorescent light buzzing so loud you can hear it through the wall.
Kid in the chair. Can't be more than nineteen.
He's crying. Not loud. Just leaking.
The detective doesn't comfort him. Doesn't sit down. Doesn't hesitate.
He slides a charge sheet across the table and says three words.
Work this off.
The kid nods. Doesn't look up. Just nods.
The detective turns to you afterward.
He doesn't explain anything. Doesn't need to.
You already understood.
Your turn.
Same booking room. Same chair. Same fluorescent buzz.
This one won't look at you. Stares at the table like there's something written on it.
There isn't.
Hands shaking.
Keeps asking if this clears it. If this is all they have to do.
You don't answer that part.
Work this off or do the time. That's the pitch.
You explain what it means. Carefully. Slowly, like it matters that they understand.
One buy. That's it. One buy and the charge disappears.
They stop shaking. Just for a second.
You mean it when you say that.
You actually mean it.
You send them in the next day.
No wire. No body recorder. No training session beforehand.
No extraction plan.
Nobody back at the precinct even knows this is happening.
They walk toward the house alone. You watch from the parking lot.
Engine running. Phone in your hand. The dashboard clock reads nine fourteen.
The streetlight above you buzzes. You notice it.
Fifteen minutes pass. Maybe twenty. You don't time it.
They come back. Shaking. Worse than before.
But they came back. That's the thing.
You file the bust report that night. Type it up at the precinct.
Their name isn't in it. Not once. Nowhere in the system.
It's like they didn't exist at all.
Your sergeant reads the report the next morning. Nods.
Commendation letter shows up in your file by Friday.
Nobody asks where the arrest came from. Nobody checks.
The system doesn't ask.
You own them.
That's the phrase. Not one they taught you in the academy.
Not something anyone says out loud to anyone else.
Just something you think when you look at the list.
Clinical. Like inventory.
You check your phone. Not the department one.
You look around before you open it.
Second one's faster. Much faster.
Smaller charge this time. Bag of weed tucked in a coat pocket.
You don't even explain the deal anymore. You just say it.
Work this off.
Three words. They nod. Same rhythm every time.
Third one. You barely remember the booking.
Fourth. Different face. Same chair.
You tap the phone screen once. Just to scroll the list.
The list keeps growing.
First week, you remember their names.
Third week, you remember their charges.
By the second month, you remember the weight of what they carried.
You don't remember when that changed.
The department knows about six of them.
Six registered informants. Proper files. Proper oversight forms signed in triplicate.
The other eight live on the second phone.
No files. No forms. No supervisor signature. No safety protocol.
No one knows they exist.
If something goes wrong with any of them - and you know things go wrong - there's nothing on paper that connects back to you.
They were never yours.
One of them starts calling you at two in the morning.
Not to report anything. Not to give a name or a location.
Just to ask if they're safe.
You sit in the dark with the phone against your ear.
You don't know what to say to that. So you say something about it being fine.
They start bringing names you didn't ask for.
People from their block. People who owe money. People with warrants.
Trying to stay useful. Handing you people so you keep handing them time.
Trying to stay alive.
You check the phone before the department one now.
Stopped looking around when you open it.
Stopped pretending it's separate from the job.
They come in one afternoon.
Same chair. But different this time.
They don't look scared anymore.
They look tired. The kind of tired that isn't about sleep.
I think I'm done.
You look at them. You don't say anything.
You reach across the table. Slide the charge sheet back.
The original one. The one from the first night.
The one that never went away.
They stare at it. Same paper. Same ink.
Then they pick up the pen.
They ask when this ends.
Not angry. Not demanding. Just asking.
You say: after this one. You're clear.
They look at you. First time in weeks they actually look at your face.
Something in their eyes that you recognize. Or don't want to.
You already know that's not true.
You knew before you said it.
Work this off.
No explanation anymore. No pitch. Three words.
Next person. Same chair. Same sheet on the table.
You stop remembering the chair.
You stop seeing faces.
You see output.
They call at two in the morning again.
The night before.
You look at the screen. Their number. The prepaid buzzing on the nightstand.
You roll over.
You don't answer.
New target comes in the next day. File on your desk by noon.
You open it. Scan through the pages. The usual charges. The usual neighborhood.
Except the margin note.
Weapon notation. Known to carry.
You close the file. Sit there for a second.
Then you stand up and walk to the booking room.
You don't tell them.
The phone rings later that night. Late.
Not the department phone.
The other one.
You pick it up and hold it to your ear.
You wait for breathing.
There is none.
The line stays open.
You don't hang up.
You hold the phone against your ear and wait.
Thirty seconds. A minute. Maybe longer.
Your jaw tightens. You're not sure when that started.
Nothing.
You put the phone down on the desk.
Open the messages. Scroll to their thread. Delete it.
Go to contacts. Find the number. Remove it.
Pull the folder from the bottom drawer. The one without a case number.
Close it.
There's a water stain on the ceiling tile above your desk.
You notice that.
You look at the phone one more time.
Thirteen contacts.
You still have thirteen.
They were never yours.
That's what you tell yourself.
Or maybe you don't tell yourself anything.
You own them.
Not a figure of speech anymore.
A new detective starts riding along the next month.
Young. Polished shoes. Reminds you of the academy.
They watch you flip someone in the booking room.
Same chair. Same charge sheet on the table. Same three words.
Afterward, in the hallway, they ask how you do it.
You say: don't ask. Tell.
They flip their first one alone the next week.
Your words. Your phrasing.
Your pitch.
They ask you one night if this is how it's supposed to work.
You don't answer.
You reach into your jacket. Pull out the phone. Hand it to them.
Next week they don't ask anymore.
Thirteen numbers.
You remember their charges. Not their faces.
You reach into your pocket. Habit.
The phone's not there. You gave it away.
Your hand still reaches.
Someone new sits down.
Same chair.
Same charge sheet. Same offer.
You already know what they'll say.
You own them.
You don't remember when that stopped being a figure of speech.
